The surgeon prepares for a posterior vaginectomy but sets up for a standard low anterior resection. In the pelvis, the loop of sigmoid colon is visibly adhered to the surrounding structures. The uterus is suspended to facilitate access.
The surgical team sets up for a standard low anterior resection, examining the pelvic area where the loop of sigmoid colon is visibly adhered to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection, planning to take the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) along with the lymph node-bearing tissue. A lymph node is visible, retained with the specimen.

The surgeon performs right-sided total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, transitioning to posterior dissection. The camera angle is adjusted from three to two views, facilitating visualization of the lower dissection area.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum from the posterior aspect of the vagina, preparing for a potential partial vaginectomy. The peritoneum is removed from the cul-de-sac area, which is notably adhered, requiring a wider dissection rim than usual. The vagina is visible, highlighting the challenging area of the procedure.

The peritoneum is removed from the cul-de-sac, revealing fibrotic tissue likely due to radiation. The surgeon identifies a tumor and observes its invasion into surrounding tissues, using improved retraction to enhance visibility. The dissection proceeds circumferentially, with attention towards the vaginal area, amidst fibrotic tissue likely resulting from radiation.
The surgeon continues the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, moving posteriorly below the previously challenging area, without needing to go full thickness into the vagina.

The surgeon decides to use a stapler for the anastomosis below the tumor due to the difficulty of sewing from above. The complete total mesorectal excision (TME) is continued down to the pelvic floor using a vessel sealer, which provides hemostatic benefits and allows for grasping and removal of more tissue at once.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) down to the pelvic floor, using a vessel sealer for hemostatic tissue dissection and grasping. A stapler is used to perform the coloanal anastomosis, ensuring a good margin below the tumor.

The surgeon performs pelvic dissection, followed by stapling. A fan and steel incision is made, and a wound protector is placed to extract the specimen. The anvil is positioned, and the robot is re-docked for the anastomosis.

The surgeon focuses on the pelvic dissection, having previously completed the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and full mobilization of the splenic flexure to facilitate tension-free anastomosis.

The surgeon staples the anterior rectum using the robotic arm, ensuring the staple line is well below the tumor. The surgeon uses a vessel sealer on the anterior rectum to palpate from below, ensuring they are well below the tumor. The stapling is performed from arm three, which is designated for the ileostomy site, using two firings of the stapler.

The surgeon examines the pelvic dissection, noting the stapled area is flush with the levators. Margins along the vaginal area with adherence have been taken, and frozen sections returned negative. The surgical field is prepared for the anastomosis.

The surgeon manually places the EEA stapler and brings the spike out, carefully palpating the vagina to avoid accidental inclusion. The robotic arms are released, and the surgeon aligns the EEA stapler with the anvil to perform the anastomosis. A leak test is then conducted to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis.
